definition: Context rot is when AI tools give worse answers in message 30 than message 3—because they remember everything you said but lose track of what you need now.

definition: Context rot is when AI tools confidently cross-contaminate contexts—giving you client B's numbers in client A's report, or Monday's architecture decision in Friday's debugging session.

By company #4, Claude suggests Company B's revenue figures in Company D's update and Company C's cost-cutting playbook for Company A—each requiring 15 minutes to detect and rewrite.

After the 3rd context switch, AI accuracy drops 15% (Chroma Research: discontinuous degradation). With 8 AI-assisted decisions per person per day, this creates 0.75 contaminated outputs daily per person, each taking 20 minutes to detect and fix. 25 people × 1.25 hours weekly × $95/hour = $154,375 annually in rework.
inevitability: Every new context multiplies cross-contamination risk non-linearly: 5 clients create 10 possible mix-ups, 10 clients create 45. As companies adopt AI for more use cases, across more people juggling more simultaneous projects, the contamination surface grows faster than usage—success with AI makes this worse, not better.

Append-only accumulation guarantees rot; more context retained means more noise in every future inference.

definition: Context rot is when AI tools can't retain company-specific knowledge across sessions—forcing you to re-teach the same policies, bugs, and decisions every conversation, or risk contaminating one task with details from another.

cost_model: 12 tickets per day × 3 minutes per re-explanation = 36 minutes daily = 3 hours weekly per engineer. inevitability: Every new support ticket, sales call, or project task starts in a clean session with no memory—or pollutes an existing session with irrelevant history. As teams adopt AI for more workflows, the number of context switches per day multiplies: a support engineer goes from 5 tickets to 15, a sales team from 3 calls to 10. definition: Context rot is when AI tools confidently cite your own outdated data back to you—last month's pricing, last quarter's org chart, or an API you deprecated three days ago—because they have no way to mark old information as superseded.

definition: Context rot is when AI tools mix context from unrelated projects in a shared conversation history, causing recommendations for Project A to be contaminated by constraints, priorities, or architectures from Project B.

definition: Context rot is when AI tools have no memory of evolving technical standards across code review sessions, forcing repeated re-explanation while simultaneously contaminating guidance with outdated architectural decisions.

definition: Context rot is when AI tools get worse at their job as you use them more, because accumulating conversation history degrades accuracy before the context window is even 25% full.
manifestation: An Engineering Manager at a 140-person SaaS company uses Claude Projects to track architecture decisions across a 6-month AWS-to-Railway migration. By month 3, the project context hits 220K tokens (22% of the 1M window). Claude starts suggesting database patterns explicitly rejected in month 1, recommending Postgres extensions incompatible with Railway that were ruled out in earlier sessions, requiring the EM to re-explain decisions every third conversation.
cost_model: 6 engineers × 3 times/week × 18 min re-explaining already-settled decisions = 5.4 hours/week = 280 hours/year at $95/hr loaded cost = $26,600/year. Plus: 4 incidents where Claude suggested a rejected pattern that made it to code review, requiring rollback = 14 hours rework each = 56 hours at $95/hr = $5,320. Total: $31,920/year for one project.
inevitability: Every AI interaction adds tokens, and the more you use AI successfully, the faster you approach the degradation threshold. @dbreunig measured performance collapse at 20% of Opus 4.6's window—meaning 80% of advertised capacity is unusable. As teams adopt AI for more workflows, they hit this ceiling faster, not slower.
counter_narrative: "Just use Claude's 1M token context window" assumes more capacity solves retention, but @dbreunig measured "SIGNIFICANT decrease in performance at tokens > 20% consumed on Opus 4.6"—meaning at 200K tokens, the remaining 800K tokens actively degrade accuracy. @shao__meng documented this as attention dispersion where "older irrelevant content starts to distract from current task." Bigger windows make the problem worse, not better.
